subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Andrew Morton asked naive questions about the patch's implementation, specifically whether the WB_start_dontcache bit is properly cleared when a writeback flusher is kicked and whether the wb_check_start_dontcache() handler correctly handles the case where there are no dirty pages to write back.

Reviewer Andrew Morton questioned whether memory is properly cleaned up after generic_write_sync() returns, ensuring it's not still dirty or reclaimable.

The reviewer questioned whether the patch's change would prevent a buildup of dirty, unreclaimable pagecache, and asked if there is any mechanism to limit this buildup.
sentiment_signals: lack of clear objection or suggestion
reviews.raw_body:
Naive questions...

On Sun, 26 Apr 2026 07:56:08 -0400 Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

---

So in the current case, when generic_write_sync() returns, all that
memory is written back and clean&reclaimable (or freed?), yes?

---

Whereas after this change, that pagecache is probably still dirty,
unreclaimable, waiting for the flusher to do its thing?

So is there potential that the system will get all gummed up with
dirty, to-be-written-soon pagecache?  Is there something which limits
this buildup?

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary:
The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the IO submission timing in the writeback path, explaining that the current implementation submits I/Os before returning but doesn't wait for completion.

The author acknowledges that the current DONTCACHE I/Os do not wait for completion, but notes that this patch defers I/O submission to a flusher thread, which should eventually take care of business. The author does not indicate a need for further revision.

The author addressed Andrew Morton's concern about the patch potentially causing writers to be limited by traditional VM limits instead of the backing device throughput, explaining that this is actually a desired outcome and that the peak pagecache usage was higher but still less than with normal buffered I/O. The author suggests considering going back to submitting writes inline when the number of dirty pages is high, but notes that this could have negative performance effects.
reviews.raw_body:
No. Before returning, it submits the I/Os for the portion that it wrote
rather than leaving it to the flusher to take care of things, but it
doesn't wait for the I/Os to complete.

---

Correct, but that's sort of the case today too since DONTCACHE I/Os
don't wait for the completion. With this change we're just deferring
the I/O submission to the flusher thread (which should hopefully soon
wake and take care of business). If the flusher thread can't keep up,
then eventually balance_dirty_pages() will kick in and start slowing
things down.

---

Today in this situation, the writers are limited by the backing device
throughput. Once the I/O submission queues are full, then the DONTCACHE
writers end up stacking up on those. With this change, the writers will
be more limited by traditional VM limits in this situation. 

In the test runs I did, the peak pagecache with DONTCACHE writes was
higher than with the unpatched version but still considerably less than
with normal buffered I/O. That's the cost of deferring the I/O
submission to the flusher.

One thing we could consider is going back to submitting the writes
inline when the number of dirty pages is high. But, that could have a
detrimental effect on performance too.

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledges that the mixed-mode benchmark results show decreased read throughput due to increased write throughput, and suggests there are multiple possible solutions to address this issue.

The author acknowledges that the patch introduces tradeoffs, but believes it's a win overall due to DONTCACHE being relatively new and having a small blast radius. They suggest considering kicking off writeback earlier in the normal buffered case as well.
reviews.raw_body:
Forgot to comment on this part earlier...

This is the "mixed-mode" (dontcache writes + buffered reads). I played
with a bunch of different settings under nfsd, and those settings
turned out to perform the best with this benchmark.

I suspect what's happening is that the increase in write throughput
from writing via the flusher thread is crowding out reads. So, read
throughput suffers in this test from that. There are a number of ways
we could probably make that more fair.

---

I think it's a win overall. As with anything writeback-related, it's a
game of tradeoffs. The good news is that DONTCACHE is still fairly new
and not many applications are using it yet, so the blast radius from
any change here should be rather small.

As a side note: I've long thought that we in general wait too long to
kick off writeback with normal buffered I/O, particularly with modern
memory sizes. DONTCACHE gives us a place to experiment with this
scheme, but we may want to think about kicking off writeback earlier in
the normal buffered case too.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox expressed surprise that the patch's solution for 'noisy neighbour' problem doesn't address his initial expectation of concurrent DONTCACHE and buffered writes, suggesting a different scenario than what the patch aims to solve.
reviews.raw_body:
hm.  This wasn't what I thought of when I thought of "noisy neighbour".
I'd have process A doing DONTCACHE writes to file A and process B doing
normal buffered writes to file B.

2026-04-26.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path and into a background flusher. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that uses targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback, preserving I/O batching while reducing latency. The patch also adds a new writeback reason for tracing visibility and targets the correct cgroup writeback domain. Performance results show significant improvements in sequential write throughput and latency, as well as random write throughput and latency.

reviews.author: Ritesh (IBM)
reviews.summary:
The reviewer noted that the writeback flusher writes back all dirty pages, regardless of type, and suggested documenting this change in the commit message or man page to clarify the behavior for RWF_DONTCACHE writes.

Reviewer requested testing of parallel buffered and don't cache writes, noting that the patch series impacts this scenario.

Reviewer Ritesh (IBM) requested clarification on test scenario details, specifically pointing out inconsistencies in file size used for single-client tests versus multi-client tests, and suggested using fio's built-in aggregation capabilities instead of parsing results manually.

Reviewer Ritesh requested an explanation for the significant performance improvement (5x) observed on NFS compared to local filesystems, indicating that he is not familiar with the NFS side and would appreciate a clarification.
reviews.raw_body:
Yup, so, we wakeup the writeback flusher, which will write those many
"number" of dirty pages. Those dirty pages written by writeback, can be
of any type though, can be DONTCACHE or normal (non-dontcache) dirty
pages. IIUC, writeback doesn't distinguish between them while writing.


IMO, what we could also include in the commit msg is why is this above
approach taken? IIUC, that is because, by writing NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY
pages, it still reduces the page cache pressure and still reduces the
amount of work that the reclaim has to do, even though some of those
pages maybe non-dontcache pages, in case if there was a parallel
buffered write in the system.


Also should the following change be documented somewhere? Like in Man
page maybe? i.e.
Earlier RWF_DONTCACHE writes made sure that those dirty pages are
immediately submitted for writeback and completion would release those
pages. But now, in certain cases when there is a mixed buffered write in
the system, those dontcache dirty pages might be written back after a
delay (whenever the next time writeback kicks in).
However for RWF_DONTCACHE reads, it should not affect anything.

---

Can we please also test parallel buffered writes and dontcache writes? 
Since this patch series definitely affects that.

BTW - adding these numbers in the commit msg itself is much helpful.

---

Can you please help describe this test scenario if possible.. In above
you mentioned we are writing file_size as 2x RAM_SIZE. But your
multi-client tests says something else..

local num_clients=4
+	mem_kb=$(awk '/MemTotal/ {print $2}' /proc/meminfo)
+	client_size="$(( mem_kb / 1024 / num_clients ))M"

Also the multi-writer case is spawning parallel fio jobs, and then
parsing and aggregating the bandwidth results instead of using fio to
spawn multiple parallel threads... which is ok, but a bit wierd.
Why not let fio do the aggregate bandwidth, and latency calculation
instead?

---

Nice :)
Some explaination here of why 5x improvement with NFS compared to local
filesystems please?
(I am not much aware of NFS side, but a possible reasoning would help)

-ritesh

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary:
Author confirms that the feedback from Jan and HCH about using a flusher kick is being addressed, as it was their suggested approach.

The author is addressing a concern about the documentation of IOCB_DONTCACHE in the preadv/writev manpage, stating that the current description accurately reflects the behavior and does not need to be updated after this change.

Author clarified that the patch only affects DONTCACHE writes, not normal buffered writes, and asked for clarification on what specific test is being requested.

The author acknowledged that the fio testsuite needs to be reworked and agreed to take a closer look at it, indicating a fix is planned.

The author is addressing a concern about the patch's impact on NFS writes, suggesting that the scattered nature of these writes causes performance issues due to competing threads submitting jobs to the queue.
reviews.raw_body:
Correct. This was the approach that Jan and HCH suggested in the
responses to the last posting.

---

Looks like DONTCACHE is documented in the preadv/writev manpage. Here's
the current blurb about writes:

    Additionally, any range dirtied by a write operation with RWF_DONT‐
    CACHE  set  will  get kicked off for writeback.  This is similar to
    calling  sync_file_range(2)  with  SYNC_FILE_RANGE_WRITE  to  start
    writeback on the given range.  RWF_DONTCACHE is a hint, or best ef‐
    fort,  where  no hard guarantees are given on the state of the page
    cache once the operation completes.

I don't think this verbiage is invalid after this change. Kicking off
writeback is still just a hint, like it was before. We could mention
about how that I/O can compete with regular buffered I/O, but it seems
a bit like we're adding info that will just be confusing for users.

---

To be clear, this only affects DONTCACHE, not normal buffered writes,
but I guess you're referring to the fact that DONTCACHE and buffered
writes can compete now.

Can you clarify specifically what you'd like me to test here? Are you
saying you want me to test parallel and buffered writes together at the
same time (i.e. make them compete?).

I should be able to do that for the local benchmarks, but nfsd's iomode
settings are global and that won't be possible there.

---

That's what I get for asking Claude to roll a testsuite. I'm not that
well-versed in fio, but that makes sense. I'll have a look at reworking
it along those lines.

---

I suspect that it's because of the "scattered" nature of nfsd writes.
When the client sends a write to nfsd, we wake a nfsd thread to service
it. So, if there are a lot of writes operating in parallel, they all
get done in the context of different tasks.

My hunch is that this I/O pattern (writing to same file from a bunch of
different threads), particularly suffers from the DONTCACHE inline
write behavior. The threads all end up competing to submit jobs to the
queue and that causes the performance to fall off sharply.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing Matthew Wilcox's concern about the patch's benchmarking methodology, specifically that it was only tested on NFS and not a local file system. The author explains that they initially tested both reads and writes to the same setting but later experimented with different modes for better performance.
reviews.raw_body:
Originally, I was benchmarking this via nfsd and only later did I add
the suite for local benchmarks. With nfsd, setting the iomode affects
all reads or writes.

So initially, I had it testing them with both reads and writes set to
the same setting, but then later I decided to play with different modes
for reads and writes. The best performing one was buffered reads +
dontcache writes. It's possible a mix of different modes will be better
on a local fs.

I can't easily do a benchmark like you're suggesting with nfsd, but it
should be possible on a local benchmark. I'll see what I can come up
with.

Thanks,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara noted that using a per-node stat (NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY) to determine the amount of dirty folios for writeback may not accurately reflect the actual bandwidth, especially when different cgroups have varying amounts of DONTCACHE writes and normal writes. He suggested changing the stat to be per-bdi_writeback instead.
reviews.raw_body:
So this will end up splitting NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY folios among per-cgroup wb
structures based on their writeback bandwidth. This is a reasonable thing
for global writeback where the bandwidth more or less corresponds to the
amount of dirty folios. However with DONTCACHE I expect big differences in
among NR_DONTCACHE_DIRTY among different cgroups not necessarily
corresponding to wb throughput. In particular if you do DONTCACHE writes
from one cgroup and normal writes from another cgroup this will
systematically underestimate the amount needed to write by a factor of
about two.

So I think the stat should be a per bdi_writeback one (instead of a per node
one) which would avoid the need to split the value to wb here.

								Honza


-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

2026-04-27.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path and into a background flusher. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that uses targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback, preserving I/O batching while reducing latency. The patch also adds a new writeback reason for tracing visibility and targets the correct cgroup writeback domain. Performance results show significant improvements in sequential write throughput and latency, as well as random write throughput and latency.

reviews.author: Ritesh (IBM)
reviews.summary:
The reviewer noted that dontcache marked pages can be delayed from being evicted from the page cache when there is a parallel buffered writer dirtying other page cache pages, potentially impacting performance in certain workloads.

Reviewer Ritesh pointed out that the patch's performance benefits are not evident in baseline cases, and suggested evaluating scenarios where buffered writers dirty page cache pages, which can overflow RAM size, to see if dontcache shines in those situations.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where the WB_start_dontcache bit is already set, which could lead to unexpected behavior and requested a check for this condition.
reviews.raw_body:
The reason I am thinking of this is: dontcache marked pages, gets
evicted from page cache after they are written back. But this patch
series can now delay that from happening when there is a parallel
buffered writer dirtying page cache pages. Because of the reasons we
already discussed...

Note that, this may not be a workload which matters in the real world,
but I was thinking, it will be good to know the impact if any, of such
workload with this patch series (parallel buffered and dontcache
writers).

---

... If we see the performace of buffered and dontcache in baseline case,
then we don't see dontcache doing any good. Even the patched version is
just slightly better compared to buffered case.

But IIUC, dontcache should really shine in cases where we have buffered
writers dirtying the page cache pages which can overflow the RAM size
[1]. The reason why dontcache should show benefit there is, because we
don't see any page cache pressure, since after writeback the pages gets
evicted. Also earlier in the unpatched version, the I/O submission
happens immediately in the same context.

So, I guess, isn't it better to evaluate those scenarios as well with
the patched version - since this series affects those code paths now?

[1]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20241110152906.1747545-11-axboe@kernel.dk/

---

Thanks!

-ritesh

2026-04-28.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path and into a background flusher. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that uses targeted dirty tracking to limit the scope of expedited writeback, preserving I/O batching while reducing latency. The patch also adds a new writeback reason for tracing visibility and targets the correct cgroup writeback domain. Performance results show significant improvements in sequential write throughput and latency, as well as random write throughput and latency.
